In a 2018 study of 400 Kenyan school aged children, researchers were able to diagnose malaria with 100% sensitivity based on volatile biomarkers in the skin (molecules that cause odors). And the volatile biomarker signature of those with symptomatic and asymptomatic disease differed significantly. Thus introducing a possible new diagnostic test for the disease.[310]

Other animals

[edit]

While none of the main four species of malaria parasite that cause human infections are known to have animal reservoirs,[311] P. knowlesi is known to infect both humans and non-human primates.[51] Other non-human primate malarias (particularly P. cynomolgi and P. simium) have also been found to have spilled over into humans.[312] Nearly 200 Plasmodium species have been identified that infect birds, reptiles, and other mammals,[313] and about 30 of them naturally infect non-human primates.[314] Some malaria parasites of non-human primates (NHP) serve as model organisms for human malarial parasites, such as P. coatneyi (a model for P. falciparum) and P. cynomolgi (a model for P. vivax). Diagnostic techniques used to detect parasites in NHP are similar to those employed for humans.[315] Malaria parasites that infect rodents are widely used as models in research, such as P. berghei.[316] Avian malaria primarily affects species of the order Passeriformes, and poses a substantial threat to birds of Hawaii, the Galapagos, and other archipelagoes. The parasite P. relictum is known to play a role in limiting the distribution and abundance of endemic Hawaiian birds. Global warming is expected to increase the prevalence and global distribution of avian malaria, as elevated temperatures provide optimal conditions for parasite reproduction.[317]

PMID 31887217.

Sources

[edit]
  • Schlagenhauf-Lawlor P (2008). Travelers' Malaria. PMPH-USA. ISBN 978-1-55009-336-0.
  • Webb JL (2008). Humanity's Burden: A Global History of Malaria. Cambridge University Press. doi:10.1017/CBO9780511808401. ISBN 978-0-521-67012-8.
  • Guidelines for the treatment of malaria. World Health Organization. 2015. hdl:10665/162441. ISBN 978-92-4-154912-7.
  • Malaria Eradication: Benefits, Future Scenarios and Feasibility: A Report of the Strategic Advisory Group on Malaria Eradication. World Health Organization. 2020. hdl:10665/331795. ISBN 978-92-4-000367-5.
  • World Malaria Report 2022. World Health Organization. 2022. hdl:10665/365169. ISBN 978-92-4-006489-8.

Further reading

[edit]
  • Barber MA (1929). "The history of malaria in the United States". Public Health Reports. 44 (43): 2575–87. doi:10.2307/4579430. JSTOR 4579430.
  • Bynum W, Overy C, eds. (1998). The Beast in the Mosquito: The Correspondence of Ronald Ross and Patrick Manson. Rodopi. ISBN 978-90-420-0721-5.
  • Harrison G (1978). Malaria: Mosquitoes, Malaria and Man. A History of the Hostilities since 1880. Dutton. ISBN 0-525-16025-6. OCLC 3169237. a major worldwide scholarly survey online
  • Jarvis B (29 July 2019). "How Mosquitoes Changed Everything". The New Yorker. Archived from the original on 7 August 2019. Retrieved 8 August 2019.
  • Karunaweera ND, Silva NH (2022). "Malaria". In Parija SC, Chaudhury A (eds.). Textbook of Parasitic Zoonoses. Singapore: Springer. pp. 151–161. doi:10.1007/978-981-16-7204-0_14. ISBN 978-981-16-7204-0.
  • Li Q, Liu T, Lv K, Liao F, Wang J, Tu Y, et al. (2025). "Malaria: past, present, and future". Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 10 (1): 188. doi:10.1038/s41392-025-02246-3. PMC 12170910. PMID 40523953.
  • Malpede M (2023). "Malaria and economic activity: Evidence from US agriculture". American Journal of Agricultural Economics. 105 (5): 1516–42. doi:10.1111/ajae.12363.
  • Poser CM, Bruyn GW (1999). An Illustrated History of Malaria. Parthenon. ISBN 1-85070-068-0. OCLC 537843745. online review of this book
  • Qidwai T, ed. (2024). Falciparum Malaria: Diagnostic Tools, Therapeutic Advances, and Future Opportunities. Academic Press. ISBN 978-0-323-95328-3.
  • Watts SJ (1999). "6. Yellow Fever, Malaria and Development: Atlantic Africa and the New World, 1647 to 1928". Epidemics and history: disease, power, and imperialism. Yale University Press. pp. 213–268. ISBN 0-300-08087-5. JSTOR j.ctt1nq8qw. OCLC 55142577.
  • Williams LL (1963). "Malaria Eradication in the United States". American Journal of Public Health and the Nation's Health. 53 (1): 17–21. doi:10.2105/AJPH.53.1.17. PMC 1253858. PMID 14000898.
  • "Tightening the handle on malaria". Nature Methods (Editorial). 16 (4): 271. April 2019. doi:10.1038/s41592-019-0390-2. PMID 30923375. A day dedicated to raising awareness of the disease is a good opportunity to ask how far malaria research has come and which methods are needed for further breakthroughs.
  • Warrell DA, Gilles HM (2019) [2002]. Essential Malariology (4th ed.). London: CRC Press. ISBN 978-0-367-39616-9.
  • WHO (2010). Guidelines for the Treatment of Malaria (PDF) (Report) (2nd ed.). World Health Organization. ISBN 978-92-4-154792-5. Archived from the original (PDF) on March 11, 2010.
  • Organization WH (2023). WHO Guidelines for Malaria. World Health Organization. hdl:10665/373339.
